topic: Tracing the epistolary networks of Roman provincial governors during the Crisis of the Third Century — how correspondence maintained imperial cohesion amid political fragmentation and rapid succession.
outline_hint: Title slide: 'Letters of Loyalty — Epistolary Networks and Imperial Cohesion, 235–284 CE'
Historical context: the Crisis of the Third Century — succession instability, provincial secession, external threats
Sources and methodology: Pliny the Younger's letters as model, the Augustan correspondence archive, papyrological evidence
Network mapping: Mermaid diagram of governor-to-emperor and governor-to-governor letter routes across key provinces
Case study 1: Gaul and Britannia — Postumus' breakaway and the correspondence gap
Case study 2: Palmyra — Odaenathus and the diplomatic letter as substitute for military deployment
Quantifying fidelity: formulaic loyalty language in official dispatches (KaTeX table of frequency distributions)
The cursus publicus under stress — infrastructure of imperial communication and its breakdown
Argument synthesis: epistolary practice as a technology of governance, not merely record-keeping
Conclusions and open questions — toward a network-theoretic model of Roman imperial resilience

topic: The intellectual debts of the French Revolution's Declaration of the Rights of Man — tracing how Rousseau's general will, Locke's natural rights, and Montesquieu's separation of powers shaped its articles.

A seminar discussion of Virginia Woolf's 'To the Lighthouse,' examining subjective time, stream of consciousness technique, and the structural parallel between the novel's three sections and Lily Briscoe's completing her painting.
outline_hint: Title: 'Finishing the Picture — Form, Time, and Vision in To the Lighthouse'
Woolf's biographical context: St Ives, Leslie Stephen, and the kernel of the novel
The Window: domestic time, perception and the 'little language' of consciousness
Time Passes: impersonal duration — decay, war, and the silence between
The Lighthouse: return, reconciliation, and Lily's 'vision' as artistic culmination
Discussion prompts: Is the novel a painting? Can fiction 'finish' what life leaves incomplete?
Further reading and closing image — Vanessa Bell's Studio at Charleston

topic: How Homeric Greek oral-formulaic composition worked — demonstrating Milman Parry's thesis through repeated type-scenes and formulaic epithets from the Iliad, with close readings of key passages.
style_hints: pedagogical, methodical, text-heavy with Greek passages
